elements.sent-2411-001: During the Z-Pinch fusion process, electric current is injected into liquid metal from the plasma column, generating Lorentz forces that deform the liquid metal's free surface.
elements.sent-2411-002: Modeling this phenomenon is essential for assessing the feasibility of using liquid metal as an electrode wall in fusion devices.
elements.sent-2411-003: Traditionally, such problems, where liquid metal is exposed to electromagnetic forces, are modeled using magneto-hydrodynamic (MHD) formulation, which is more suitable for cases without external electric current penetration into liquid metals.
elements.sent-2411-004: MHD formulation typically models situations where liquid metal flows in the presence of an external magnetic field, with the initial magnetic field known and evolving over time via the magnetic induction equation.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, in Z-Pinch fusion devices, the electric current penetrates and traverses through the liquid metal, necessitating numerical calculations for the initial magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, the deformation of the liquid metal surface alters the current path's geometry and the resulting magnetic field, rendering traditional MHD formulations unsuitable.
elements.sent-2411-007: This work addresses this issue by directly solving Maxwell's equations, instead of the magnetic induction equation, in combination with Navier-Stokes equations, making it possible to predict the magnetic field even when the fluid is in motion.
elements.sent-2411-008: The Maxwell equations are solved in potential formulation alongside Navier-Stokes equations using a finite volume numerical method on a collocated grid arrangement.
elements.sent-2411-009: This proposed numerical framework successfully captures the deformation of the liquid metal's free surface due to the applied electric current.
elements.sent-2411-010: The quest for fusion energy aims to deliver a clean, abundant, and sustainable power source that can meet global energy demands without the environmental drawbacks of fossil fuels.
elements.sent-2411-011: Among the different methods for achieving controlled thermonuclear fusion, magnetic confinement fusion, particularly the Z-Pinch, stands out as a compelling and promising area of research.
elements.sent-2411-012: Z-Pinch involves passing a large current through a plasma column, creating a very strong magnetic field that helps confine and compress the plasma to conditions suitable for fusion.
elements.sent-2411-013: Within this plasma, Deuterium and Tritium, two hydrogen isotopes, are key players in one of the most common methods to achieve fusion.
elements.sent-2411-014: When these isotopes are compressed and heated to millions of degrees, their nuclei undergo fusion, producing helium and a neutron.
elements.sent-2411-015: This fusion reaction releases a substantial amount of energy (17.6 MeV), which can be captured and harnessed.
elements.sent-2411-016: The reaction is represented by the following equation: \begin{equation}{}^2_1D + {}^3_1T \rightarrow {}^4_2He (3.5 \text{ MeV}) + {}^1_0n (14.1 \text{ MeV})\end{equation} After the fusion reaction, the plasma emits high-energy neutrons that play a crucial role in the reactor's energy production.
elements.sent-2411-017: When these neutrons strike the blanket surrounding the fusion reactor, they transfer their kinetic energy, significantly heating the blanket.
elements.sent-2411-018: The blanket, as the immediate interface with the high-energy plasma and neutrons, endures constant bombardment, leading to severe stress and radiation damage.
elements.sent-2411-019: This results in wear, degradation, and potential impurity introduction into the plasma.
elements.sent-2411-020: Consequently, the need for frequent maintenance and replacement of the blanket impacts the reactor's efficiency and operational lifespan.
elements.sent-2411-021: Using a liquid wall in fusion reactors offers a promising alternative to traditional solid walls.
elements.sent-2411-022: Unlike solid materials, which can crack and degrade over time, a liquid wall continuously renews its surface, effectively handling the damage from radiation and heat.
elements.sent-2411-023: The flowing liquid metal absorbs and dissipates the intense heat from plasma and neutron impacts more efficiently than solid materials.
elements.sent-2411-024: Although using a liquid metal wall in Z-Pinch fusion reactors offers potential advantages, its practical implementation requires a thorough understanding of the liquid metal's free surface behavior.
elements.sent-2411-025: This behavior is affected by Z-Pinch plasma currents and various electromagnetic forces present in the fusion environment.
elements.sent-2411-026: Ensuring stable free surface conditions in the liquid metal walls of Z-Pinch devices is crucial for effective fusion, as any instability can lead to plasma contamination and disrupt the fusion process.
elements.sent-2411-027: This paper aims to develop a mathematical model that describes how Z-Pinch plasma currents influence the free surface behavior of the liquid wall.
elements.sent-2411-028: Various studies have explored the dynamics of fusion liquid walls under these forces across different reactor designs.
elements.sent-2411-029: In a key study, Brannick \cite{brannick2022theory formulated an analytical model to describe the wave generation in liquid metal induced by the magnetic pressure from a plasma column.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In Z-Pinch fusion, electrical currents flow from the plasma column into the surrounding liquid metal.
elements.sent-2411-031: This current flow through the liquid metal produces a spatially varying magnetic pressure, which in turn generates a Lorentz force.
elements.sent-2411-032: Brannick's analytical model examines how this Lorentz force displaces the free surface of the liquid metal electrode, leading to wave formation.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the study conducted by Sun et al. \cite{sun2023magnetohydrodynamics , the effects of magneto-hydrodynamic (MHD) drag on flowing liquid metal plasma-facing components were investigated.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Although liquid metal is a promising technology for fusion reactors, MHD drag reduces the flow speed in conducting liquids.
elements.sent-2411-035: Experiments were conducted using an open-channel device with either insulating or conducting walls.
elements.sent-2411-036: The results indicated that the average channel flow speed decreased with the use of conducting walls and with increasing transverse magnetic field strength.
elements.sent-2411-037: Liquid metal plasma-facing components (LM-PFCs) often rely on the Lorentz force to ensure liquid metal adheres to the interior surfaces of fusion reactors.
elements.sent-2411-038: In a study by Hvasta et al. \cite{hvasta2017demonstrating , the potential of electromagnetic control for managing free-surface liquid metal flows was examined.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The researchers introduced electrical currents into the liquid metal as it traversed a uniform magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-040: This method effectively controlled both the flow velocity and depth, consistent with theoretical predictions, thereby demonstrating the capabilities of electromagnetic control.
elements.sent-2411-041: A major concern with implementing liquid metal plasma-facing components in fusion reactors is the risk of liquid metal being ejected into the plasma column during the fusion process \cite{fiflis2016free .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Shallow water theory is applied to simulate wave propagation and evaluate the stability of liquid metal under plasma exposure.
elements.sent-2411-043: This theory indicates that ejections are more likely to happen at the edges, where waves collide with the walls, rather than from the center of the trenches.
elements.sent-2411-044: Many studies analyzing the stability of liquid metal surfaces in fusion plasma have neglected the presence of the plasma sheath region, characterized by large electric fields and strong ion flows.
elements.sent-2411-045: Holgate et al. \cite{holgate2018electrohydrodynamic address this gap by performing a linear perturbation analysis of the liquid-sheath interface to determine the stability conditions for liquid metal surfaces.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Narula et al. \cite{narula2005study explored the behavior of liquid metal film flow under magnetic fields relevant to fusion environments through a 3D numerical simulation of two-phase electrically conducting liquids.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The simulations predicted an increase in liquid metal film thickness due to magneto-hydrodynamic body forces and captured the tendency of the liquid metal to move away from the side walls.
elements.sent-2411-048: Xiujie et al. \cite{xiujie2008mhd studied magneto-hydrodynamic effects on liquid metal film flows for plasma-facing components in fusion technology.}
elements.sent-2411-049: They identified three MHD phenomena—retardant, rivulet, and flat film flow—concluding that flat film flow is the most suitable for plasma-facing components due to its resistance to disruptive MHD effects.
elements.sent-2411-050: Somboonkittichai et al. \cite{somboonkittichai2023surface formulated a mathematical model employing the Lagrange equation to characterize the deformation of a viscous liquid surface in a magnetized fusion plasma, utilizing Rayleigh’s method.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The study concludes that strong magnetic fields, crucial for confinement in fusion devices, primarily govern instability.
elements.sent-2411-052: It suggests reevaluating the use of static planar free liquid surfaces in fusion devices due to their natural instability under these conditions.
elements.sent-2411-053: Beyond research on fusion liquid walls with free surfaces \cite{murugaiyan2024modeling,murugaiyan2019response , studies on two-phase magneto-hydrodynamics (MHD) provide significant insights.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The investigation of two-phase MHD flows is complex, and this area of research is notably less developed compared to studies on single-phase MHD phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-055: A numerical framework for multiphase magneto-hydrodynamic flows with large property gradients is studied by \cite{flint2021magneto .}
elements.sent-2411-056: The framework captures Melting, solidification suitable for advanced manufacturing scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-057: Righolt et al. conducted a comprehensive study exploring the analytical solutions for one-way coupled magneto-hydrodynamic free surface flow \cite{righolt2016analytical .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The research examined the behavior of a conductive liquid layer influenced by surface tension, gravity, and Lorentz forces.
elements.sent-2411-059: Kharicha et al. \cite{kharicha2015experimental studied the deformation of a liquid metal's free surface under electric currents using experimental and numerical methods.}
elements.sent-2411-060: A cylindrical container filled with liquid metal had electric currents applied through central copper electrodes.
elements.sent-2411-061: At low current densities, the interface shifts downward due to the Lorentz force's pinch effect.
elements.sent-2411-062: At high current densities, the increased flow causes further interface displacement driven by a Bernoulli mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-063: Research into the stability of fusion liquid walls has extensively investigated the complex electromagnetic forces present in fusion environments.
elements.sent-2411-064: Many studies have utilized traditional magneto-hydrodynamic (MHD) equations, particularly focusing on the magnetic induction equation to model the magnetic field's evolution.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, Z-pinch scenarios pose unique challenges when plasma current directly interacts with liquid metal.
elements.sent-2411-066: The MHD approach requires the magnetic field generated by the current as an initial condition, which is often unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-067: This issue stems not only from the lack of analytical solutions even for simpler geometries but also from the deformable nature of liquid metals.
elements.sent-2411-068: To overcome these challenges, this research develops a numerical solver that directly solves Maxwell's equations to calculate the magnetic field induced by the current.
elements.sent-2411-069: The developed numerical solver models the behavior of a quiescent liquid metal layer subjected to an external electric current by directly solving Maxwell's equations alongside the Navier-Stokes equations.
elements.sent-2411-070: This acts as a model for plasma current interacting with liquid metal walls in a Z-pinch fusion device.
elements.sent-2411-071: A simplified computational model is used, where a wire carrying an electric current replaces the plasma column.
elements.sent-2411-072: The current flows through the wire, enters the liquid metal, and then exits through the wall of the cylindrical container, which is grounded.

elements.section-2411-003: Computational Domain: Wedge Formulation for Axi-Symmetric Problems
elements.sent-2411-101: All the numerical simulations presented in this paper are axisymmetric simulations performed using wedge mesh.
elements.sent-2411-102: This approach is particularly well-suited for problems where field do not change in the circumferential direction \cite{weller1998tensorial .}

elements.sent-2411-103: In axially symmetric simulations using wedge meshes \cite{weller1998tensorial , the computational domain is divided into a series of wedges, each representing a segment of rotation around the central axis.}
elements.sent-2411-104: This method facilitates the modeling of the physical system in a 2D plane, thereby reducing the computational load compared to full 3D simulations.
elements.sent-2411-105: A notable advantage of the wedge mesh approach for axially symmetric simulations is that it allows the use of a Cartesian coordinate system for the computations.
elements.sent-2411-106: The wedge method for simulating axially symmetric systems involves modeling the geometry as a thin wedge \cite{weller1998tensorial .}
elements.sent-2411-107: This wedge typically has a small angular span, often less than 5°, and a thickness of a single cell.
elements.sent-2411-108: In these simulations, the boundaries are defined as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:2c .}
elements.sent-2411-109: The back boundary represents the inner surface of the cylinder, whereas the front boundary corresponds to its outer surface.
elements.sent-2411-110: The top and bottom boundaries align with the upper and lower surfaces of the cylinder, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-111: Lastly, the left and right boundaries correspond to the axially symmetrical surfaces of the cylinder.
elements.sent-2411-112: The governing partial differential equations are discretized using the Finite Volume Method \cite{jasak1996error, weller1998tensorial, darwish2016finite .}
elements.sent-2411-113: This method utilizes a grid configuration where all fluid variables are stored at the center of the cells, adhering to a collocated scheme.
elements.sent-2411-114: For more details, refer to Jasak's PhD thesis \cite{jasak1996error .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Finite volume discretization uses the integral form of differential operators \cite{haber2014computational, pletcher2012computational, darwish2016finite , which is derived using Gauss's divergence theorem.}
elements.sent-2411-116: This approach transforms the governing differential equations into a matrix equation represented as $AX=B$.
elements.sent-2411-117: The discretization process separates terms into explicit and implicit components, with explicit terms incorporated into vector $B$ and implicit terms included in matrix $A$.
elements.sent-2411-118: Consequently, the algebraic representation for each cell following discretization is formulated as: \begin{equation}a_P \phi_P + \sum_{N=1}^{nfaces} a_N \phi_N = b_P \label{eq:2.1}\end{equation} Where $N$ represents neighboring cells ($E,W,N,S$) and $nfaces$ represents number of cell faces.
elements.sent-2411-119: Divergence of vector results in scalar quantity.
elements.sent-2411-120: The integral definition of divergence operator can be written as \cite{jasak1996error,haber2014computational . \begin{equation}\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \int \limits_{V} \nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} \,dV\end{equation} Here, $\mathbf{A}$ is any vector quantity and $\Delta V$ is the volume of the computational cell.}
elements.sent-2411-121: The volume integral can be converted to surface integral using Gauss divergence theorem \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} = \frac{1}{\Delta V}\int \limits_{\partial \Omega} \mathbf{A}\cdot \mathbf{dS} = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \,\, \sum_{nfaces} \mathbf{A}_f \cdot \, S \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, $ S$ is cell face area vector and $\mathbf{A _f$ is the face value of the vector $A $.}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation} \nabla \cdot A = \frac{1}{\Delta V}\int \limits_{\partial \Omega} A\cdot dS = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \,\, \sum_{nfaces} A_f \cdot \, S \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-124: The volume integral can be converted to surface integral using Gradient theorem \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \nabla \phi = \frac{1}{\Delta V}\int \limits_{ \partial \Omega} \phi \, \mathbf{dS} = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \,\, \sum_{nfaces} \phi_f \, \, S \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, $\phi_f$ is face value of the scalar quantity $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-125: Discretization of time derivative can be carried out by employing either the Euler or the Backward Euler method.
elements.sent-2411-126: Euler Scheme : The Euler scheme is represented by the following equation ( \cite{weller1998tensorial,jasak1996error ): \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \phi }{\partial t} = \frac{\phi - \phi^o }{\Delta t}\end{equation} Here, $\phi^o$ represents the value from the previous time step.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Applying the Euler scheme to each control volume yields the discretized algebraic equation: \begin{equation}\left( \frac{\partial \phi }{\partial t} \right)_P = \frac{1}{\Delta t} \left( \phi_P -\phi^o_P \right)\end{equation} The coefficients and source terms for the matrix equation are then calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} a_P = \frac{1}{\Delta t} \qquad a_N = 0 \qquad b_P = \frac{\phi_P ^o}{\Delta t} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Backward Euler Scheme : The Backward Euler scheme is expressed by ( \cite{weller1998tensorial,jasak1996error ): \begin{equation}\frac{\partial \phi }{\partial t} = \frac{1}{\Delta t} \left( \frac{3}{2} \phi - 2 \phi^o + \frac{1}{2} \phi^{oo} \right)\end{equation} Here, $\phi^{oo $ represents the value from the second old time step.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Applying the Backward Euler scheme to each control volume yields the discretized algebraic equation: \begin{equation}\left( \frac{\partial \phi }{\partial t} \right)_P = \frac{1}{\Delta t} \left( \frac{3}{2} \phi_P - 2 \phi^o_P + \frac{1}{2} \phi^{oo}_P \right) = \frac{1}{2 \Delta t} \left( 3 \phi_P - 4 \phi^o_P + \phi^{oo}_P \right)\end{equation} The coefficients and source terms for the matrix equation are then calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} a_P = \frac{3}{2 \Delta t} \qquad a_N = 0 \qquad b_P = \frac{1}{2 \Delta t} \left( 4 \phi_P ^o - \phi_P ^{oo} \right) \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: For orthogonal meshes, cell face gradients can be represented as ( \cite{weller1998tensorial,jasak1996error ): \begin{equation}\nabla \phi_f \cdot \, S = |S| \frac{\phi_N - \phi_P }{|d|}\end{equation} In this context, $d$ represents the vector denoting the distance from the center of cell P to the center of the neighboring cell N ( \cite{jasak1996error ).}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Discretization of Laplacian operator becomes \begin{equation}\nabla \cdot \sigma \nabla \phi = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \,\, \sum_f \sigma_f |S| \frac{\phi_N - \phi_P }{|d|} \\\end{equation} Comparing with the matrix equation and gathering the coefficients yields: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} a_P = -\frac{1}{\Delta V} \sum_f \sigma_f \frac{|S| }{|d|} \qquad a_N = \frac{1}{\Delta V} \sum_f \sigma_f \frac{|S| }{|d|} \qquad b_P = 0 \end{aligned}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-154: To find the current density, Ohm's law is applied as $\mathbf{J = - \sigma \nabla \Phi$.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Since the current is purely axial, only the axial component of the vector potential, $A_z$, needs to be considered: $\nabla \cdot \nabla A_z = - \mu_0 J_z$.
elements.sent-2411-157: In cylindrical coordinates, the Laplacian of $A_z$ is expressed as: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left(r \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2} \frac{\partial^2 A_z}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 A_z}{\partial z^2} = - \mu_0 J_z\end{equation} Considering the symmetry, the vector potential depends only on the radius, simplifying the equation to: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left(r \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial r}\right) = - \mu_0 J_z \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left(r \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial r}\right) = - \mu_0 r J_z\end{equation} Integrating the equation twice yields: \begin{equation}A_z = - \left(\frac{\mu_0 J_z }{4}\right) r^2+ C_1 \ln r + C_2\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-159: To ensure that the magnetic field is continuous at the inner surface of the wire, the vector potential $A$ must also be continuous across this boundary since $B = \nabla \times A$.
elements.sent-2411-160: This implies that the gradient of the vector potential should be zero at this surface, leading to the boundary condition: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial A}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=r_i} = 0\end{equation} Furthermore, to confine the magnetic field within the wire and ensure that it decays to zero as we move away from the wire, the vector potential should decay to zero as well.
elements.sent-2411-161: Mathematically, this means that the vector potential should be zero at the outer surface of the wire, leading to the boundary condition: \begin{equation}A\bigg|_{r=r_o} = 0\end{equation} Applying the zero gradient boundary condition at the inner surface and Dirichlet boundary condition at the outer surface yields: \begin{equation}A_z = \left(\frac{\mu_0 \sigma V_o }{4l}\right) (r^2 - r_o^2) - \left(\frac{\mu_0 \sigma V_o r_i^2 }{2l}\right) ( \ln r - \ln r_o )\end{equation} The magnetic field can be calculated using the curl of the vector potential: \begin{equation}\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A} =\left( \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial A_\theta}{\partial z}\right)\hat{r} + \left(\frac{\partial A_r}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial r}\right)\hat{\theta} + \frac{1}{r}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(rA_\theta) - \frac{\partial A_r}{\partial \theta}\right)\hat{z}\end{equation} The only non-vanishing component is ($A_z$ depends solely on $r$): \begin{equation}B_\theta = - \frac{\partial A_z}{\partial r} = - \frac{\mu_0 \sigma V_o }{4a r} (r^2 - r_i^2)\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-165: For the case of a hollow cylinder with inner radius $r_i$ and outer radius $r_o$, and with the outer surface at zero potential and the inner surface at potential $V_o$, the analytical solution is derived as follows: Following similar approach to the axial current case, the electric potential can be found using the Laplace equation in cylindrical coordinates. \begin{equation}\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left(r \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2} \frac{\partial^2 \Phi}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \Phi}{\partial z^2} = 0\end{equation} Since the current is purely along the $r$ direction, the Laplace equation simplifies to: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left(r \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial r}\right) = 0\end{equation} Integrating twice with respect to $r$ yields $\Phi = C_1 \ln r + C_2$, where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are constants.
elements.sent-2411-166: Applying the boundary conditions, the electric potential is obtained as follows: \begin{equation}\Phi = \frac{V_o}{\ln (r_i/r_o)} \ln (r/r_o)\end{equation} To calculate the current density, Ohm's law can be used as before $\mathbf{J = - \sigma \nabla \Phi$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: In cylindrical coordinates, the gradient of the potential is given by: \begin{equation}\nabla \phi = \hat{r} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} + \hat{\theta} \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} + \hat{z} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\end{equation} Since $\Phi$ is only a function of $r$: $ \displaystyle J(r) = - \sigma \frac{\partial \Phi {\partial r \hat{r}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Substituting the previously derived expression for $\phi$, the result is: \begin{equation}J(r) = - \frac{\sigma V_o}{r \ln (r_i/r_o)}\end{equation}.

elements.paragraph-2411-003: Algorithm for Solving the Maxwell-Navier-Stokes Equation for Electrically Driven Flows
elements.sent-2411-187:
Solve the Maxwell Equation: Calculate the electric potential by solving the Poisson equation as follows:  egin{equation}
abla \cdot \sigma 
abla \Phi - 
abla \cdot \left( \sigma U 	imes Bight) = 0\end{equation} In this equation, the term $
abla \cdot \left( \sigma U 	imes Bight) $ is an explicit source term.
elements.sent-2411-188: Momentum Predictor: Estimate the velocity field $\mathbf{U}$ by solving the momentum equation.
elements.sent-2411-189: The pressure gradient term $\nabla p$ and the Lorentz force term $\mathbf{J \times \mathbf{B}$ are incorporated as explicit source terms based on the initial conditions or values from the previous time step.}
elements.sent-2411-190: Construct the H Operator: The discretized momentum equation can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}a_P U_P + \sum_{N=1}^{nfaces} a_N U_N = b_P\end{equation} Considering Rhie-Chow interpolation \cite{jasak1996error,rhie1983numerical,yi2016improved , the momentum equation is discretized excluding the pressure gradient. \begin{equation}a_P U_P + \sum_{N=1}^{nfaces} a_N U_N = b_P - \nabla p\end{equation} rewriting as : \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad $a_P U_P = H[U] - \nabla p $ where: \begin{equation}H[U] = b_P - \sum_{N=1}^{nfaces} a_N U_N\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-192: Solving this equation provides the pressure field necessary to correct the velocity field.
elements.sent-2411-193: Correct the Velocity Field: Update the velocity field using the pressure field from the last step: \begin{equation}U = \frac{1}{a_{P}} H[U] - \frac{1}{a_{P}} \nabla p\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-194: Correct the Velocity Flux: Compute the corrected velocity flux at the cell faces using Rhie-Chow interpolation: \begin{equation}\mathbf{U}\cdot \mathbf{S}_f = \left(\frac{1}{a_{P}} \mathbf{H}[\mathbf{U}]\right)\cdot \mathbf{S}_f- \left(\frac{1}{a_{P}} \nabla p\right)\cdot \mathbf{S}_f\end{equation} Note that this is the velocity flux used in the discretization of divergence term discussed in section \ref{subsec:4.4 .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Iterate steps 3 to 6 until convergence is achieved.
elements.sent-2411-196: Once convergence is achieved, move on to the next time step and repeat the process starting from step 1.

elements.sent-2411-224: An electric potential difference is applied across the cylinder's inner and outer cylindrical surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-225: Current ($\mathbf{J $) flows radially within the liquid metal due to applied potential difference as shown in Figure \ref{fig:5.17 .}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Radial current flowing through the liquid metal generates azimuthal magnetic field ($\mathbf{B}$) in accordance with Ampere's law.
elements.sent-2411-227: The magnetic field due to radial current is presented in Figure \ref{fig:5.21 .}

elements.sent-2411-228: The radial current, in conjunction with the azimuthal magnetic field, produces a Lorentz force $\mathbf{J \times \mathbf{B}$, whose direction is downward as determined by the cross product $\mathbf{J \times \mathbf{B}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-229: The cross-sectional area through which current flows increases radially, given by $2 \pi r d$.
elements.sent-2411-230: Here, $r$ represents the radial distance, and $d$ denotes the depth of the liquid metal.
elements.sent-2411-231: As the radial distance $r$ increases, the cross-sectional area also increases.
elements.sent-2411-232: Consequently, the current density must decay in the radial direction to satisfy the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-233: As the current density decreases radially, the magnetic field also decreases radially.
elements.sent-2411-234: Additionally, the magnetic field also decreases axially as the enclosed current decreases in the axial direction \cite{fleisch2008student,griffiths2017introduction,jackson1999classical , starting from the free surface .}
elements.sent-2411-235: The enclosed current can be conceptualized as the number of current density lines crossing a hollow cylindrical surface at a specific radial distance with different heights.
elements.sent-2411-236: A cylinder with its base coinciding with the bottom surface of the container will have fewer current lines crossing it if its height is much smaller than the depth of the liquid metal.
elements.sent-2411-237: Conversely, if the height of the cylinder approaches the depth of the metal, and its top surface coincides with the free surface, the number of enclosed current density lines is maximum.
elements.sent-2411-238: Consequently, in the axial direction, the magnetic field is stronger near the free surface, whereas in the radial direction, it is stronger near the inner cylindrical surface.
elements.sent-2411-239: Thus, a strong magnetic field is observed at the intersection of the interface with the inner cylindrical surface.
elements.sent-2411-240: This two dimensional magnetic field can be visualized in Figure \ref{fig:5.21 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Since both the current density and magnetic field decay in the radial direction, the resulting net downward Lorentz force $\mathbf{J \times \mathbf{B}$ is stronger near the inner cylindrical surfaces and decays radially.}
elements.sent-2411-242: Consequently, the liquid metal is pushed downward near the inner cylindrical surface with a magnitude stronger than at other radial positions.
elements.sent-2411-243: Consequently, fluid pressure builds up in response to this body force, leading to deformation of the liquid metal's free surface as shown in Figure \ref{fig:5.15 .}
elements.sent-2411-244: As the liquid metal deforms, the current density lines and resulting magnetic field adjust to the shape of the liquid metal.
elements.sent-2411-245: The simulation is stopped before the liquid metal loses contact with the inner cylindrical surface.
elements.sent-2411-246: An attempt has been made to numerically simulate the free surface deformation of a fusion liquid wall, caused by the injected current from Z-pinch plasma.
elements.sent-2411-247: The approach is not only applicable to fusion wall scenarios but broadly to situations involving current injection into any conducting liquid.
elements.sent-2411-248: The development of the final solver involved a systematic approach, beginning with solving Maxwell's equations, then integrating them into the single-phase Maxwell-Navier-Stokes framework, and finally extending to the two-phase Maxwell-Navier-Stokes equations.
elements.sent-2411-249: The potential formulation of Maxwell's equations is solved using a finite volume numerical method and then compared with the derived analytical solution for an axially current-carrying wire.
elements.sent-2411-250: The numerical solutions are found to be in close agreement with the analytical solutions.
elements.sent-2411-251: The study then extends to radial current flow in a cylindrical domain, where the current flows radially in an annular cylinder from the inner cylindrical surface to the outer cylindrical surface.
elements.sent-2411-252: This simulation is particularly relevant as it closely relates to the current flow in the fusion liquid wall's case, aiding in understanding the boundary conditions for simulating electromagnetic fields in such scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-253: In the case of an axial current-carrying wire, the current density is uniform.
elements.sent-2411-254: However, for radial current flow, the current density decays radially.
elements.sent-2411-255: Additionally, while the magnetic field in the axial current-carrying wire varies only radially, in the radial current case, it changes both radially and axially.
elements.sent-2411-256: In scenarios where liquid metals flow in the presence of electromagnetic fields, secondary electrical currents and magnetic fields are induced.
elements.sent-2411-257: To accommodate this, Maxwell's equations in their potential form are extended to moving conductors, utilizing Ohm's law for such moving conductors.
elements.sent-2411-258: Maxwell's equations for moving conductors are then solved in conjunction with the Navier-Stokes equations, which include a Lorentz body force term, arising due to electromagnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-259: The combined Maxwell-Navier-Stokes equations are formulated for two specific scenarios: one involving purely applied current, and the other a combination of applied current and an external magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-260: To validate the Maxwell-Navier-Stokes system for single-phase flows, electrically driven flows in an annular cylinder is simulated.
elements.sent-2411-261: This test case references Khalzov's dissertation \cite{khalzov2008equilibrium , where the evolution of the magnetic field is solved using toroidal field components and poloidal flux functions.}
elements.sent-2411-262: In contrast, this study solves the same problem using the potential formulation of Maxwell's equations in Cartesian coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-263: The setup of the case is as follows: radial currents flow in an annular cylinder from the inner cylindrical surface to the outer cylindrical surface.
elements.sent-2411-264: Additionally, an axial magnetic field permeates the entire domain.
elements.sent-2411-265: The interaction between the radial currents and the axial magnetic field generates an azimuthal Lorentz force, causing the metal to spin.
elements.sent-2411-266: This phenomenon is simulated, and the resulting angular momentum profile shows good agreement with the analytical solution derived from Khalzov's work \cite{khalzov2008equilibrium .}
elements.sent-2411-267: The final study focuses on simulating the two-phase Maxwell-Navier-Stokes equations with the goal of including free surface deformation, representing a fusion liquid wall model scenario.
elements.sent-2411-268: In this model, current from a wire, analogous to the plasma column in a Z-pinch, flows radially through the liquid metal layer.
elements.sent-2411-269: However, the wire or plasma column itself is not part of the model; only the radial current flowing into the liquid metal layer is considered.
elements.sent-2411-270: Unlike the previous study on electrically driven flow in an annular channel, there is no external magnetic field present to rotate the flow.
elements.sent-2411-271: In this scenario, the radial current flow results in a non-uniform current density along the radius, decaying in the radial direction.
elements.sent-2411-272: Consequently, the magnetic field generated by this current also decays radially.
elements.sent-2411-273: Furthermore, there is an axial variation in the magnetic field, as the enclosed current changes axially, leading to a two-dimensional variation in the magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-274: This spatially varying magnetic field produces a net downward force within the liquid metal.
elements.sent-2411-275: The resulting Lorentz force leads to a buildup of fluid pressure, causing deformation of the interface, a phenomenon demonstrated through numerical simulation.. \bibliographystyle{unsrt.}
